fund.name: HSBC Equity Savings Fund - Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: Your stability anchor. Holds ~30% stocks, ~35% arbitrage, ~35% bonds — very low volatility (6% yearly swings vs 14%+ for stock funds). Yet it qualifies for equity tax rates: long-term gains taxed at just 12.5% vs 30% on debt funds. The sweet spot for conservative investors in the 30% tax bracket.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core holding: ~70% stocks, ~30% bonds. Grows faster than pure debt with less volatility than pure stock funds. Equity-taxed — long-term gains above ₹1.25L taxed at 12.5% instead of 30%. This tax advantage alone could save ₹20L+ over 10 years.

fund.name: Nippon India ETF Gold BeES
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Safety net: gold rises when stocks fall, cushioning your portfolio during crashes. Largest gold ETF in India (₹53,346 Cr AUM). Note: gold is slab-taxed at 30%, but at 15% allocation the diversification benefit outweighs the tax cost.

fund.name: HSBC Aggressive Hybrid Fund - Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: Second hybrid from a different fund house (HSBC vs ICICI). Same equity-tax advantage. Different stock-picking approach reduces the risk of both funds making the same mistakes.

fund.name: quant Multi Asset Allocation Fund-GROWTH OPTION-Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Diversification across stocks, gold, and debt in one fund. Outstanding 23.8% over 5 years. Slab-taxed (30% on gains), but at 10% allocation post-tax returns still beat pure debt.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential NASDAQ 100 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Geographical diversification — America's top 100 companies (Apple, Microsoft, Google). Low 0.20% fee. At 10%, a 30% US drop means just 3% hit to total portfolio. Equity-taxed. Primarily for the long-term inheritance goal.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Inheritance growth engine. Low-cost index fund (0.20% yearly fee) tracking 150 mid-sized companies. Modest 5% allocation — enough to boost long-term returns, not enough for sleepless nights. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Professional flexibility — manager shifts between large, mid, and small companies. Gives active management without small-cap volatility. ₹32,020 Cr fund with 20%+ 5-year track record. Equity-taxed.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU (free). Always choose Direct plans — saves 0.5-1%/year vs Regular.

Step 2: Tag your contingency — keep ₹50L earmarked for healthcare (₹30L from Equity Savings + ₹20L from ICICI hybrid). Only touch for genuine medical emergencies.

Step 3: Deploy ₹2 Cr lumpsum in 2-3 tranches over 2-4 weeks. Don't stretch beyond a month.

Step 4: Start ₹1,00,000/month SIPs across the 8 funds as listed. Automate on the 5th of each month.

Step 5: Choose Growth option (not IDCW) for all funds. Reinvests profits for compounding.

Step 6: Activate 10% annual step-up SIP.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Verify SIPs are running and lumpsum deployed. Don't check returns.
After 1 year: Note any fund underperforming its category by 5%+. Don't switch yet.
At age 60 (retirement, 2030): Plan withdrawal strategy — start with 4-5% of corpus/year. Shift mid-cap and flexi-cap into hybrid funds for stability.
Every year post-retirement: Adjust withdrawal based on market conditions.
Age 65+: Reduce mid-cap and NASDAQ to 2-3% each, move into hybrid funds.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check if any fund has drifted 5%+ from target. Sell the overweight, buy the underweight. This forces 'sell high, buy low' automatically. After retirement, take monthly income from whichever fund is above target — withdrawals double as rebalancing. Don't rebalance more than once a year.
plan.rationale:
Minerva, you're 55, conservative, and 5 years from retirement with ₹2 Cr saved. Three key insights drive this plan:

1. YOUR 30% TAX SLAB CHANGES EVERYTHING. Pure debt funds return 7-8% pre-tax but only 4.9-5.6% after 30% tax — barely above inflation. Equity-taxed funds (hybrid, equity savings, index) tax long-term gains at just 12.5% above ₹1.25L, yielding ~9-10% post-tax. This could save ₹20-30L in taxes over the decade.

2. YOUR ₹2 CR DOES THE HEAVY LIFTING. Even at 8-9%, it adds ₹16-18L/year. You don't need to chase risky returns. Effective equity stays at ~45-50%, with 15% gold as a safety cushion.

3. INHERITANCE (₹4 CR) NEEDS TIME, NOT RISK. At ~11% blended returns, ₹2 Cr becomes ~₹5.7 Cr in 10 years — exceeding ₹4 Cr even without SIPs. The SIPs fund your retirement income.

No small-cap (per your preference), no pure debt (tax-inefficient at 30% slab), spread across 5 fund houses.
risks: Markets can drop 15-20% temporarily. With ~50% equity, a severe crash could shrink ₹2 Cr to ₹1.6-1.7 Cr. Scary but normal — recovery takes 2-3 years. Gold cushions this.
Interest rate changes affect the bond portion inside hybrid funds. Stock gains partly offset this.
10% NASDAQ allocation means US market risk. At only 10%, even a 30% US drop = 3% hit to total portfolio.
Inflation erodes purchasing power. At 11% returns and 6% inflation, real return is ~5%. ₹5.7 Cr in 10 years buys what ₹3.2 Cr buys today. Some growth allocation is necessary.
Healthcare costs can spike. Get standalone health insurance (₹25-50L cover) to protect the contingency fund.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total SIP by 10% each year. ₹1L/month becomes ₹1.10L/month next year. Adds ~₹25-30L to final corpus over 10 years.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The equity allocation includes two index funds (ICICI Prudential NASDAQ 100 and Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150), which together constitute 15% of the total equity allocation (10% + 5% of total portfolio = 15% equity exposure). However, the remaining equity exposure (HDFC Flexi Cap, hybrids with active equity components) is actively managed. Since only index funds count as passive, and they make up a minority of the equity sleeve, the score is moderate but not high.

turnover_score: Rebalancing is advised only once per year, and only if allocations drift by more than 5%. Post-retirement withdrawals are used as a rebalancing mechanism. No tactical shifts or frequent trading is recommended, indicating a largely buy-and-hold orientation. Hence, the score is high on passivity.

cost_emphasis_score: Expense ratios are mentioned for some funds (e.g., 0.20% for index funds), and the plan emphasizes using Direct plans to save 0.5-1% annually. However, cost is not the dominant selection criterion — tax efficiency, diversification, and risk reduction are emphasized more. Several funds have 1.0% expense ratios without critique. Thus, cost matters but is secondary.

research_vs_cost_score: There is no deep stock-specific or fund-manager analysis. Fund selection is based on structure (hybrid, index), tax treatment, and broad diversification rather than manager skill or company fundamentals. However, active funds like HDFC Flexi Cap are included based on track record and size, implying some research. The rationale leans more toward structural and tax logic than deep research, so the score is moderately in favor of passive.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 10-year horizon with clear long-term triggers (retirement at 60, adjustments at 65+). SIPs are automated with step-up, and investors are told not to check returns frequently. The inheritance goal is explicitly long-term. This reflects strong long-term orientation.

portfolio_activeness_score: The portfolio includes two genuine index funds (NASDAQ 100 and Nifty Midcap 150), but also several actively managed hybrid and flexi-cap funds. The hybrids (HSBC, ICICI, Quant) have active mandates with stock picking, and HDFC Flexi Cap is actively managed. While index funds are present, the majority of the portfolio relies on active management. Risk metrics are missing, but category labels and rationales suggest active strategies dominate. Thus, the portfolio is more active than passive, but not fully conviction-driven active, so the score is neutral.

pqs.reasoning:
The investment plan demonstrates strong alignment with Minerva McGonagall's pre-retirement, conservative profile and multi-generational goals. The 10-year horizon and ₹2Cr corpus are appropriately addressed with a 45-50% effective equity exposure, prioritizing capital preservation while allowing growth for inheritance. The plan avoids small-caps as per preference and emphasizes hybrid and equity-taxed funds to optimize post-tax returns in the 30% tax bracket.

Diversification is robust across asset classes (hybrid, gold, international, mid-cap, flexi-cap), geographies (US via NASDAQ), and fund houses (5 distinct AMC relationships), reducing concentration risk. The inclusion of gold (15%) and multi-asset allocation (10%) enhances portfolio resilience.

Risk-return is well-calibrated: conservative tilt via hybrid funds dominates, with only 10% in higher-risk mid-cap and NASDAQ. Projected returns (11% base) are realistic given the allocation, and downside risks (market drops, inflation, healthcare) are explicitly addressed in the rationale and risks section.

Internal consistency is excellent: the rationale, allocations, tax logic, rebalancing rules, and future glide path (via allocation_schedule) form a coherent narrative. The step-up SIP, review checkpoints, and retirement transition plan reinforce long-term discipline.

Tax efficiency is optimal: the plan fully leverages equity taxation (12.5% LTCG) by using Aggressive Hybrid, Equity Savings, Index, and Flexi Cap funds — avoiding slab-taxed debt funds despite the investor's conservative stance. It correctly acknowledges that pure debt would yield only 4.9-5.6% post-tax at 30% slab, versus 9-10% from equity-taxed hybrids. ELSS is not recommended, which is correct since the investor did not mention 80C utilization or tax-saving as a goal, and the plan focuses on post-tax return efficiency. Gold ETF and Multi Asset Allocation are slab-taxed but held at modest weights (15% and 10%) where diversification benefits outweigh tax costs — and this trade-off is explicitly justified. The rationale highlights tax savings of ₹20-30L over 10 years, showing deep tax-awareness. Thus, tax_efficiency earns a perfect score.
